proved: For N participants, the injected runner executes N independent proposals, N cyclic cross-critiques, one evidence audit, and two order-swapped judgments before an equal 2N+3-call baseline.
Calls within each phase settle before the next phase begins, and a failed phase prevents later phases from starting.
Provider, model, and slot routing fields remain outside judge and baseline payloads and outside the public adversarial run record.
The separate execution ledger records only route identity, status, sanitized error code, and usage rather than prompt or output content.
Unknown executor error messages are not copied into the public error or ledger; only a bounded explicit error code can survive.
Proposal, critique, evidence-audit, judgment, synthesis, minority, and baseline artifacts are size-checked before later phases or record assembly.
Duplicate source snapshot IDs and a pre-aborted run fail before executor invocation.
The assembled record passes the plan-relative validator, requires a pending human decision, and cannot apply a shared mutation.
The runner imports no product provider bridge and every recorded test used a synthetic injected executor.
The compiled MCP reports injected-runner-no-product-executor and retains twelve protocol-clean semantic tools.
notProved: A native batch disclosure safely authorizes the exact providers, models, content categories, source snapshots, call count, and retention policies for a panel.
A product executor calls local or remote models, records per-call provider provenance, persists run artifacts, or resumes interrupted work.
The UI configures, displays, compares, accepts, rejects, exports, or revisits an adversarial run.
Any candidate claim is correct, any citation entails its claim, or an adversarial panel outperforms a compute-matched baseline.
Live provider behavior, cost, latency, retries, rate limits, streaming, tool calls, or cancellation after a call has begun.
The synthetic fixture corpus represents real policy research tasks.
